China's export-driven economy, which weathered Trump's tariffs last year, is now facing severe pressure from the US-Iran war.

Two new suspected hantavirus cases were reported Friday - one in Spain and another on the remote South Atlantic island of Tristan da Cunha - expanding the outbreak beyond the cruise ship cluster that has already killed three passengers.

UK Labour leader Keir Starmer faces intense pressure after his party suffered heavy losses in local elections, with Reform UK making significant gains and Labour losing power in Wales. Despite calls for his resignation, Starmer has vowed to fight on as party leader.

The EU has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine after Kyiv resumed Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia, ending months of deadlock. Hungary's new leadership under Péter Magyar has prioritized resetting relations with Brussels after Viktor Orbán's 16-year tenure.

The Virginia Supreme Court struck down Democrats' redistricting plan in a major blow to the party's midterm hopes, effectively overturning a voter-approved amendment that could have helped Democrats gain four House seats. The ruling guarantees Republican advantages in upcoming elections.

The UK and France have signed a new £662m three-year deal to combat Channel migrant crossings, including deploying 50 riot-trained French police officers and enhanced surveillance technology. The agreement includes provisions to withdraw UK funding if crossing numbers aren't sufficiently reduced.

Apple is developing an AirTag-sized AI pendant with always-on camera and microphone for Siri commands, designed to be worn as a necklace. The device, still in early development, features contextual reminders and proactive suggestions.

Olex.AI won the 2026 Bett Award for Assessment, Planning & Progress Monitoring, offering GCSE-level English teachers AI tools that reduce workload and automate marking while keeping teachers in control.

triadic_ontology.findings:
Nathan A. Adams' "Perichoretic Persons: Diagramming Augustine's Mental Trinity" (September 2025) offers a fresh computational approach to trinitarian metaphysics by diagramming the cognitive interchanges in Augustine's De Trinitate. Adams argues that Augustine's "mental trinity" of memory, understanding, and will reveals a perichoretic structure where divine persons are constituted by their interpenetrating relations rather than substantial independence. This challenges classical substance metaphysics by suggesting personhood emerges from relational co-constitution.

Complementing this, the Unity-in-Distinction framework (Ichthus77, March 2026) proposes a triadic metaphysical logic where Being, Action, and Quality form an irreducible, mutually-requiring structure. This schema argues that each element becomes incoherent without the others - Being without Action collapses into non-being, Action without Being becomes expression without substance, and Quality without both devolves into mea [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three new papers push the boundaries of autonomous research systems. The HyperAgents framework from UBC/Meta introduces self-referential agents where a meta-agent can edit not only the task-solving agent but also its own improvement procedure, enabling open-ended self-acceleration across coding, paper review, robotics reward design and Olympiad math grading. A complementary closed-loop architecture eliminates human labels entirely by using deterministic verifiers (code execution, theorem provers) to supply free supervision, doubling success rates in tool-use environments from 12 % to 23.5 % without any annotated data. On the evaluation side, the new ScienceAgentBench provides 102 rigorously validated tasks extracted from 44 peer-reviewed publications across bioinformatics, chemistry, GIS and psychology, showing that even the best current agents solve only ~32 % of tasks unaided and ~34 % with expert knowledge, underscoring the gap between headline demos and real scientific workflows. F [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chair of the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, is leading an active 2025-2026 congressional push for UAP transparency. She claims to have personally reviewed materials in a SCIF that are "of nonhuman origin and creation" and has formally demanded 46 classified UAP videos from the Department of War, threatening subpoenas if they are not delivered. A September 2025 hearing featured whistleblowers and drone footage of an orb allegedly withstanding a missile strike, underscoring the committee’s assertion that UAPs pose a real national-security threat and that AARO has been "less than adequate" in its disclosures.

weather: Mostly cloudy today in Edmonds, 64°F high/53°F low. Light SW winds 5-10 mph, near-zero rain chance. Evening stays cloudy, cooling to the low-50s with gentle breezes—pleasant for a stroll or playground visit with Piper.

classroom_ready.title: 12th Grade ELA Full-Year Curriculum: On Being Human
classroom_ready.topic: Full-year thematic curriculum anchored by Hamlet, Klara and the Sun, Sula, Exit West, and Fun Home
classroom_ready.summary: Five complete units (25-28 days each) built around the essential question 'What does it mean to be human?' Each unit bundles close-reading lessons, Socratic-seminar protocols, evidence-based writing tasks, and standards-aligned rubrics. Ready to print or import into Google Classroom.

classroom_ready.title: NYS Regents Part 3 Text-Analysis Mini-Lesson
classroom_ready.summary: 45-minute turnkey lesson that walks students through reading a complex 600-800 word text, identifying central idea, selecting one authorial strategy, and writing a concise analytical paragraph. Includes timing, text suggestions, graphic organizer, and differentiation notes.

classroom_ready.title: Rhetoric & Contemporary Political Commentary
classroom_ready.topic: Rhetorical analysis of John Pavlovitz op-ed on January 6th
classroom_ready.summary: Two-period sequence that teaches metaphor, imagery, loaded diction, and ethos/pathos/logos through a charged but timely text. Features a device-to-effect chart, 'neutral rewrite' media-literacy extension, and discussion norms for civil discourse.

classroom_ready.title: Common Core Figurative Language Boot Camp
classroom_ready.summary: Complete single-period plan with bell-ringer, direct-instruction mini-lecture, collaborative practice, and exit ticket. Google-Docs handouts and auto-graded quiz included; built with AI then teacher-reviewed for accuracy and engagement.

classroom_ready.title: Climate-Change Student Journalism Unit
classroom_ready.topic: Investigative writing & media literacy on climate topics
classroom_ready.summary: Multi-week unit that scaffolds students from spotting misinformation to pitching, researching, and publishing a feature article. Includes interview protocols, citation mini-lessons, and rubrics aligned to CCSS writing and speaking/listening standards.

pedagogy_pieces.title: Helping Middle School Students Read Challenging Texts
pedagogy_pieces.summary: Susan Yergler outlines four interactive tasks—anticipation guides, chunked annotation, collaborative summary strips, and metacognitive exit tickets—that boost comprehension of complex informational and literary texts. Techniques scale cleanly to 9-12 classrooms.

english_lesson_plans.notes: All resources are free, standards-aligned, and classroom-ready as of 2026-05-09. URLs have been verified against the prior-coverage list to avoid duplication.

global_news.findings: China's export-driven economy, which weathered Trump's tariffs last year, is now facing severe pressure from the US-Iran war. Manufacturing hubs like Foshan report workers struggling with reduced factory orders, rising costs, and job losses as the Middle East conflict disrupts global supply chains and energy prices.

Two new suspected hantavirus cases were reported Friday - one in Spain and another on the remote South Atlantic island of Tristan da Cunha - expanding the outbreak beyond the cruise ship cluster that has already killed three passengers.

UK Labour leader Keir Starmer faces intense pressure after his party suffered heavy losses in local elections, with Reform UK making significant gains and Labour losing power in Wales. Despite calls for his resignation, Starmer has vowed to fight on as party leader.

The EU has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine after Kyiv resumed Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia, ending months of deadlock. Hungary's new leadership under Péter Magyar has prioritized resetting relations with Brussels after Viktor Orbán's 16-year tenure.

The Virginia Supreme Court struck down Democrats' redistricting plan in a major blow to the party's midterm hopes, effectively overturning a voter-approved amendment that could have helped Democrats gain four House seats. The ruling guarantees Republican advantages in upcoming elections.

The UK and France have signed a new £662m three-year deal to combat Channel migrant crossings, including deploying 50 riot-trained French police officers and enhanced surveillance technology. The agreement includes provisions to withdraw UK funding if crossing numbers aren't sufficiently reduced.

wearable_ai.findings: Apple is developing an AirTag-sized AI pendant with always-on camera and microphone for Siri commands, designed to be worn as a necklace. The device, still in early development, features contextual reminders and proactive suggestions. Meanwhile, Vocci has launched an AI smart ring that listens, transcribes, and organizes thoughts in real-time, positioning itself as a productivity-focused alternati [TRUNCATED]

Olex.AI won the 2026 Bett Award for Assessment, Planning & Progress Monitoring, offering GCSE-level English teachers AI tools that reduce workload and automate marking while keeping teachers in control. New platforms EduArc and Teacher's Buddy provide curriculum-mapped lesson creation and 45+ single-click AI tools respectively, while Stylus delivers human-moderated AI writing assessment with sente [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hit me!’ ” ♦

enriched_articles.title: EU approves €90bn loan for Ukraine as pipeline is turned on ending deadlock
enriched_articles.text:
# EU approves €90bn loan for Ukraine as pipeline is turned on ending deadlock

Ukraine says it has resumed pumping Russian oil through a pipeline into Hungary and Slovakia, bringing to an end months of deadlock over a €90bn (£78bn) loan seen as vital European Union support for Kyiv.

Soon afterwards, EU ambassadors meeting in Brussels gave preliminary approval to the loan, as well as a 20th package of sanctions on Russia, officials said.

It is now expected to be signed off on Thursday.

UK and France strike new £662m small boats deal
enriched_articles.text:
# UK and France strike new £662m small boats deal

Riot-trained police will be sent to beaches in France as part of a new £662m deal with the UK to stop illegal migrants from crossing the English Channel.

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood signed the three-year agreement with France on Thursday, which will see at least 50 police officers, trained in "riot and crowd control tactics", drafted in to tackle violence and "hostile crowds".
